comments.text:
👇 Everything we curated for this Amazon episode. Tours, books, history, photography, travel resources.

*📚 BROWSE THE AMAZON COLLECTION*

  ◦ Amazon jungle tours from Manaus, Iquitos, and across the basin → https://shorturl.at/2O7we
  ◦ Browse all Amazon experiences → https://shorturl.at/rpzgt

*📖 PLAN YOUR AMAZON TRIP*
  ◦ Lonely Planet Brazil 2024 edition → https://amzn.to/43yWD9p
  ◦ Lonely Planet Brazilian Portuguese Phrasebook → https://amzn.to/4u7qm4i

*📚 GO DEEPER INTO THE AMAZON*
  ◦ Tree of Rivers by John Hemming, the definitive single-volume history → https://amzn.to/49suHYg
  ◦ The Falling Sky by Davi Kopenawa, the Yanomami shaman's testimony → https://amzn.to/49uBr7Z

*🍲 BRING THE AMAZON HOME*
  ◦ D.O.M. by Alex Atala, the cookbook built on Amazon produce → https://amzn.to/4u0U7Dy
  ◦ Sebastião Salgado: Amazônia, six years of photography across twelve tribes → https://amzn.to/4nSDys5

comments.author: @tantrum153
comments.text: I’m always saddened when I see the destruction humans have caused the Amazon, humanity depends on this Great River, Forest, World…as for viewing there is so many documentaries it’s hard to stay loyal to any. I watch Free Documentary which has most dangerous  ways/roads; I just rotate thru them. I will subscribe because I love everything Amazon & all of the lesser known last wild places on earth.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That loyalty struggle is real with so much good content out there — glad the Amazon pulled you in enough to stick around here. Thank you for subscribing, and for caring about the places most people never think to worry about.

comments.author: @WhereonEarth4K
comments.text:
Over 100 groups in the Amazon have chosen to never make contact with the outside world.

The forest that hides them is shrinking every year. Do we protect their isolation — or does protection itself require the contact they rejected?

comments.text: Excellent doc but I really wish these documentaries would stop assuming the earth and it's species were millions and billions of years old. Real science has proven this to be impossible, given the fact that the Earth's rotation is slowing at a known rate and the fact that the moon is moving away from the earth at a known rate, simple mathematical calculations reveal that these outrageous assumptions of age are impossible, knock it off already!

comments.author: @DrSebby
comments.text: "The source"??? 😆 what a stupid, human concept. It's "SOURCE" is a giant region that is a rain basin. It's fed by countless outpourings. There is no specific original

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's essentially the argument the documentary makes — the river resists the question itself. Four hundred years of trying to pin down a single source, and the Amazon keeps proving it's a system, not a line. You've landed on the thesis.

comments.author: @katyeagle6494
comments.text: So fascinating to learn that 98% of the people 1000 years ago living in the Amazon were wiped out due to smallpox or other diseases. I wonder why the universe allowed that to happen it just doesn't seem like a very good plan. However, it's so fascinating to know that the healthy soil comes from the trees, not from the dirt and it is passed on down into the rivers and then enriches soil for farming. that's another reason why we should not be  clearcutting  because you can't just regrow that stuff that's hundreds of thousands of years old. I also love how the two rivers come together- the dark black river with rotting plant matter full of health. Sometimes I think we should just go backwards if we want to maintain this earth.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: You've connected the pieces exactly right... the nutrient cycle in the Amazon runs through the canopy, not the ground. Clear the trees and you lose the system, not just the timber. That's what makes clearcutting irreversible in a way most people don't realize. Thank you for watching so closely.

comments.author: @zhou11234
comments.text: Great documentary ❤

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K

comments.author: @Juleslifevlogs
comments.text: Great documentary of the Amazon. Most recent also. I also been in the Amazon in 2012 and 2016 near Iquitos and it's an amazing place especially staying in the jungle for days. I have planned on going for the 3rd time. I've always compare every forest I've been too to the Amazon nothing will ever compare it. Due to its air quality and nature sounds. It is the one of the 8th wonders in the world.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Iquitos is the usual starting point... budget lodges and local guides are available along the river. Everything runs on boat schedules. Worth researching the dry season for easier travel. Hope you make it there.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Near Iquitos twice and planning a third...  that says everything. When people who've actually been there say nothing compares, we trust that more than any research. Thank you for watching.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Exactly.... the river shifts its channels seasonally, the várzea floods and recedes, tributaries merge and separate. A map made in June can be wrong by December. It's a moving target.

comments.text: Wonderful documentary. It was a completely new information that Rubber was at the centre of economic prosperity even deep inside Amazon and there is even a huge city that was once sprawling and is still standing tall at the banks of this great river.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The rubber boom story doesn't get told enough...  a city with electric lights before most of Europe, built on latex and slave labor, gone in a decade. Glad that section landed. Thank you for watching.

comments.author: @MarizamAbdullah-mq8id
comments.text:
Long time ago, without a compass, you don't know whether you boat is going North South East West except where sunshine penetrated
the dense forest canopy overhanging the river banks!!! 😂

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's what made Father Fritz's 1707 map so remarkable... he charted the entire system by hand, navigating by sun, current, and information from indigenous communities. No GPS, no aerial view. Just observation and memory.

comments.author: @SilverSlayer23
comments.text: Very European perspective on this one. The people who made the soil fertile are the ancestors of the indigenous people of the region... they didn't disappear. Indigenous people always understood how to exist in the Americas.  Its the Europeans who never understood and fucked everything up.🤦‍♂ Even the false perception that the Amazon couldn't support cities when it supported some of the largest ancient cities on the planet is strictly based squarely within Euro-centric thought.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: A perspective worth sitting with. The dark, fertile soils and the scale of the ancient settlements are increasingly understood as deliberate human work, not accident — the people shaped that landscape rather than merely surviving it. Thank you for adding the depth.

replies.author: @chattykathie7129

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K

comments.author: @desireegoulett69
comments.text: Imagine how much more amazing, diverse, and extraordinarily beautiful our entire planet would be without humans carving and mining, cutting, and destroying entire ecosystems that kills off entire species everyday many of whom were never discovered or known.  If we were only orbs of energy with the freewill to circumnavigate the globe without any physical impact as observers only how cool would that be to see one giant nature preserve that would be a pristine and stunning earth.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's a beautiful thought! though the Amazon adds an interesting wrinkle. The terra preta research suggests that some of the forest's richness exists because people shaped it. The 227 hyperdominant species, many of them fruit and nut trees planted by civilizations now gone. Sometimes human presence made things more diverse, not less. The question might be less about presence and more about intent. Thank you for watching.
